full_response:
The given line element \( ds^2 = -dt^2 + \alpha^2 t^2 dx^2 + dy^2 + dz^2 \) can be shown to be a coordinate transformation of the Minkowski metric by introducing new coordinates \( T \) and \( X \) defined as:

\[
T = t \cosh(\alpha x), \quad X = t \sinh(\alpha x), \quad Y = y, \quad Z = z.
\]

Computing the differentials:

\[
\begin{aligned}
dT &= \cosh(\alpha x)\, dt + \alpha t \sinh(\alpha x)\, dx, \\
dX &= \sinh(\alpha x)\, dt + \alpha t \cosh(\alpha x)\, dx.
\end{aligned}
\]

Then,

\[
\begin{aligned}
-dT^2 + dX^2 &= -\left[ \cosh^2(\alpha x)\, dt^2 + 2\alpha t \cosh(\alpha x) \sinh(\alpha x)\, dt\, dx + \alpha^2 t^2 \sinh^2(\alpha x)\, dx^2 \right] \\
&\quad + \left[ \sinh^2(\alpha x)\, dt^2 + 2\alpha t \sinh(\alpha x) \cosh(\alpha x)\, dt\, dx + \alpha^2 t^2 \cosh^2(\alpha x)\, dx^2 \right] \\
&= (-\cosh^2 + \sinh^2)\, dt^2 + 2\alpha t (-\cosh\sinh + \sinh\cosh)\, dt\, dx \\
&\quad + \alpha^2 t^2 (-\sinh^2 + \cosh^2)\, dx^2 \\
&= -dt^2 + \alpha^2 t^2 dx^2,
\end{aligned}
\]

using the identity \(\cosh^2 \theta - \sinh^2 \theta = 1\). Including the unchanged \( dy^2 \) and \( dz^2 \), the line element becomes the Minkowski metric:

\[
ds^2 = -dT^2 + dX^2 + dY^2 + dZ^2.
\]

Thus, the given metric is a coordinate transformation of standard Cartesian special relativity coordinates.

\boxed{T = t\cosh(\alpha x),\quad X = t\sinh(\alpha x),\quad Y = y,\quad Z = z}

Confidence: 100%
